item.title: Meaningfully Different? How Visual Semantics Help New Ventures Achieve Optimal Distinctiveness in Online B2C Marketplaces
item.description: We examine how new ventures in online B2C marketplaces use visuals, such as product videos and images, in their strife for optimal distinctiveness, that is, being as different as conformingly possible. Building on research on cultural entrepreneurship as well as sensory marketing and perception, our work shows that visuals help new ventures orchestrate their distinct organizational identity, that is, appearing either more con- forming or different to evaluating audiences than they truly are. We use machine learning approaches from image label recognition and natural language processing on videos and images of 1,157 entrepreneurial products offered on Amazon Launchpad to analyze the effect of the visual semantics—the meaning contained in visuals—on product performance. We find that visual semantics matter most for new venture’s product performance when firm distinctiveness is low, albeit with a juxtaposing effect: While visuals should adhere to consumer expectations in terms of semantic fit, that is, the type of meaning they convey, they should deviate in terms of semantic rich- ness, that is, the extent of meaning conveyed. By offering a multifaceted perceptual perspective on the way a distinct organizational identity is visually conveyed to audi- ences, our work has important implications for research on optimal distinctiveness. For managers, our work demonstrates clear guidelines for designing and using semantics in product videos and images to appear more or less unique to consumer audiences in online B2C marketplaces.

item.title: Known for being different? Reputation, Status, and the Optimal Distinctiveness of New Ventures
item.description: We investigate how audience evaluation of new ventures’ distinctiveness changes over time, i.e., when audiences become more familiar and less uncertain about them.Using longitudinal data on 4,279 products offered by 853 new ventures over 211 weeks on Amazon Launchpad, we observe familiarity through status, i.e., new ventures increasing establishment in the respective product category, and uncertainty through their reputation, i.e., the increasingly available evidence on past performance. Building on the notion of optimal distinctiveness as the trade-off between legitimacy and differentiation, we propose that both status and reputation steepen the U-shaped effect of new ventures’ distinctiveness. However, while highly distinct ventures benefit the most, new ventures face an ever increasing “stuck in the middle” problem that becomes particularly problematic when audiences become more familiar and less uncertain about averagely distinct ventures. Our results show that audience evaluation of a new venture’s distinctiveness is not only contingent on preferences or competitive and categorical contexts, but also by evolving degrees of complementary organizational traits. From a managerial perspective, our results provide guidance on how to achieve optimal distinctiveness while evolving into more mature market actors. Managers need to consider that although they may have found a favorable degree of distinctiveness when entering the overall market this may change as their status or reputation grows.

item.title: Paying for Legitimacy? The Signalling Effect of Monetary Rewards in Innovation Contests
New conference talks
item.description:
New year, new conference season!

My recent study with Stephie has been accepted at the SASE 2022 and the AOM 2022 Annual Conference! If you want to meet up in beautiful Amsterdam and/or Seattle make sure to drop me a mail!

item.title: Welcome to my page
item.description:
New page launched!

I am very happy that I have finally found the time to compile this new site that focuses on my work as a researcher. On it, you can find announcements for talks, teaching materials, papers, data, code and everything else that has to do with my work. Also, I will share some (hopefully) nifty tricks that I learned in my ten years as an empirical researcher that utilizes large-scale secondary data.

Enjoy reading and browsing and feel free to drop me a mail in case you have any questions!

item.title: More than words! How narrative anchoring and enrichment help to balance differentiation and conformity of entrepreneurial products
All for the Money? The Limits of Monetary Rewards in Innovation Contests with Users
An Upbeat Crowd. Fast In-store Music Alleviates Negative Effects of High Social Density on Customers’ Spending
Walking Parallel Paths or Taking the Same Road? The Effect of Collaborative Incentives in Innovation Contests
